title: 荣誉附件正文｜LOA Combine（第2部分）
content:
). However, those works either 1) assume unrealistic problem settings (e.

g. , task incremental settings with boundary-aware data streams (Qin et al. 2022)) or 2) target different application scenarios (e. g. , model fine-tuning with supervised training datasets (Lin et al. 2022)). They provide highly valuable A-1 RTDS S 22 14 05 1 techniques, but none of them solve the problem that we aim to address. A key challenge towards OCPLM2 is that it is impractical to maintain all the data streams for PLMs maintenance as data volume keeps on increasing, while the storage space is limited. Motivated by the fact that the importance of each data point in the data stream varies, prior works proposed to utilize coreset selection to 1) selects the most representative and informative coreset at each iteration and trains models more cost effectively (Li, Shen, and Chen 2022), or 2) support reply-based mechanism (Yoon et al. 2022) that keeps a subset of the training examples (coreset) to be replayed later to alleviate catastrophic forgetting. However, none has explored the potential of applying coreset selection to address both data selection and anti-forgetting at the same time for better PLM maintenance over data streams. In fact, existing coreset selection methods are stateless based on a mini-batch scheme overlooking important information across batches. Furthermore, most existing coreset selection mechanisms require labelled datasets, while OCPLM2 involves only unlabeled data streams. d. Potential Impact of Proposed Research to Defence and Security: Driven by the widely deployed 5G and IoT technology, we envision that online machine learning will soon become a mandatory task for every application including defense and security domains. 5G can provide higher device density and bandwidth with low latency, which creates new opportunities in computing but increases security risks like attacks, spammers, and frauds. In this regard, the proposed online continuous PLM maintenance that is self-trained and updated with real time data streams can respond to these changes quickly and allow us to generate timely insights to end users much earlier than the existing approaches. As a concrete example, cyber threats are hard to identify and predict because the hackers that conduct these attacks often obfuscate their activity and intentions. However, they may still use publicly accessible forums discuss vulnerabilities and share tradecraft about how to exploit them. The behavior of the hacker community, as expressed in such venues, may provide insights into group’s malicious intent (Deb, Lerman, and Ferrara 2018). To this end, the proposed project can be applied together with our previous work on online sentiment classification to better predict malicious cyber events by exploiting malicious actor’s behavior via sentiment analysis of posts on hacker forums in real-time. e. Technical Description of the Seed Project: • Methodology & Approach Background and Current State Figure 1 shows a naïve deployment of OCPLM2. Specifically, the textual data stream is spitted into data batches, and we iteratively load a mini-batch for model training until one pass over each data batch.
